titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 29 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/81

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Nov. 29' P.kest of P Rs I quite approve of your proposed dis-
sipation "for the children" even tho' it probably
means your sharing a box with Lady C. I do not mind a little extravagance on
children's pleasures if it is not overdone & the
children made blasé & peevish. You might do something for the other 2
families Eds and Mab's either in the way of
Xmas presents or of a show. Grown-up presents
are off this year at least mine are. I am
distinctly alarmed at the prospect of what
Douglas has told you that I lack. I honestly
can think of nothing & already my kitbag
is bellowing, so please to not enlarge. I do
not think it pays to stint myself of essentials
whilst I am quite sure it is a mistake to
multiply non-essentials. Please tell Gen' Powell that the regular
Brigade which Gen' Nugent was so anxious to
get in place of the 107th has not proved
at all a success, & he now wants to get
the 107th back again. They however are so
dispirited by his farewell remarks to them that they
are working heaven & earth not to return. It
is such a pity. We had officers like Crozier with
plenty of experience, but Gen' N has taken the
line that they are all no good & brings in
over their heads men of 7 years service. He really does not understand how to lead
Ulstermen. They are not African niggers. Perhaps he is getting to realise this &
will try to get a little more geneal to them. I don't think he likes the way a
certain triumvirate or inner Cabinet are run-
ning things, & shall not be surprised if he
does not help to make things a success.
The contrary perhaps. The more you deal
direct with your unit committees the better
I think, but let their requests be carefully checked
as they may quite possibly put in for socks
etc which are easily obtainable form Government
sources, & which some COs are too lazy to indent
for. I forward Mater's letters tho' I fear they
may rather add to the size of the PR 's head.
However if she remembers that other letters have gone
round the family which I have not yet seen, perhaps
she will then become a proper sized PR. I leave my present quarters with some
regret they have been unusually comfortable
& I don't like a chateau Best of love to the family
but still
Your
BW WBSpender
